Francisco labor council and the
iron trades council, one of the fore
most labor leaders of the Pacific
coast, who toured the state of
Maine two years ago with Samuel
Gompers in an effort to prevent
the reelection of Congressman
Littlefield.


Sweeney says Cannon is the bull
wark of the vested interests
against which labor and the COM
mon people have stormed time and
time again in fruitless battle for
beneficial legislation. He says:


Organized labor has many rea-
sons for wishing to see Joe Can
non retired to private life. Chief
among these is that every
measure ever presented to con-
gress that savored of fair play for
the workingman has been referred
to hostile committees.


''He represents the corporations
and trusts who today practically
own the workingman body and
soul. BY his technical power of
authority he has prostituted the Of
fee of speaker and whips congress
men into accepting his arbitrary
rulings. His recent utterances on
the stump have forced the convic-
tons upon us that we must defeat
him at all hazards.


organized labor is not in poll
tics for political gain, but the flu
ture peace of our unions and indus
trial harmony in our commercial
centers is wholly dependent upon
the defeat OF Cannon for speaker"
